citation.title: Mass transport mechanisms insights of selective sodium/magnesium separation through nanofiltration membranes

content: A B S T R A C T This study investigates the selective ion separation capabilities of interfacially polymerized nanofiltration (NF) membranes, focusing on the effect of piperazine (PIP) and trimesoyl chloride (TMC) concentrations on Na+/ Mg2+ selectivity. By varying the concentrations of PIP and TMC during interfacial polymerization (IP), we altered the membranes’ physicochemical characteristics and assessed their performance. The Donnan Steric Pore Model with Dielectric Exclusion (DSPM-DE) was used to explore the correlations between the DSPM-DE input param­eters and the partitioning mechanisms and transmembrane fluxes of Na+ and Mg2+ in terms of the monomer concentrations. Our findings reveal that variations in the thickness-to-porosity ratio and the effective membrane charge density, influenced by higher PIP and lower TMC concentrations, enhance the Na+/Mg2+ separation capabilities of the membranes. This study provides a mechanistic understanding of selective Na+/Mg2+ sepa­ration in terms of PIP and TMC concentrations, elucidating the underlying transport and exclusion mechanisms. These insights are important for optimizing membrane fabrication by IP and designing NF membranes for effi­cient Na+/Mg2+ separation, which is essential due to the importance of magnesium as a critical raw material.

citation.title: Mass transport mechanisms insights of selective sodium/magnesium separation through nanofiltration membranes

content: 1. Introduction Nanofiltration (NF) is a pressure-driven membrane process that can be used to selectively retain multivalent ions while allowing monovalent ions to permeate. NF membranes achieve the selective separation of ions based on their size, charge, diffusivity, and solubility [1]. To fully exploit the separation capabilities of NF membranes and minimize en­ergy consumption, it is essential to select membranes with tailored properties [2]. Due to the similar hydrated ionic radii of Mg2+ and Na+, separation based solely on sieving or steric effects might be insufficient. Therefore, non-sieving mechanisms, such as Donnan or charge effects, become more significant, with a positively charged membrane surface being particularly effective in enhancing divalent/monovalent cation selectivity [1–4]. This underscores the importance of elucidating the role of the membrane’s characteristics and underlying ion permeation mechanisms that influence cation selectivity, particularly in the context of Na+/Mg2+ separation. In our previous study [5], we demonstrated the effectiveness of NF membranes in selectively separating NO-3 / SO2-4 ions, where dielectric exclusion and diffusion were identified as the dominant exclusion and transport mechanisms, respectively.

citation.title: Mass transport mechanisms insights of selective sodium/magnesium separation through nanofiltration membranes

content:
Despite advancements in NF membrane fabrication, a research gap remains regarding the systematic influence of monomer concentrations in interfacial polymerization (IP) on Na+ and Mg2+ separation in terms of mass transport modeling. The Donnan Steric Pore Model with Dielectric Exclusion (DSPM-DE) is a state-of-the-art model that charac­terizes the membranes based on their physicochemical features, namely the pore radius (rpore), the thickness-to-porosity ratio of the active layer (Δxe), the charge density (cx), and the pore dielectric constant (εpore) [6]. These membrane characteristics serve as the input parameters of the model, determining the extent of the exclusion mechanisms and the magnitude of the transmembrane ionic fluxes, which ultimately drive the ion selectivity [6–10]. Previous studies on cation selectivity modeling have primarily focused on the effects of NF membrane operating parameters and syn­thesis conditions [8,9,11–22]. In the context of the DSPM-DE model, variations in the intrinsic characteristics of the membranes, resulting from changes in operating parameters and synthesis conditions, are re­flected as changes in the input parameters of the model. The influence of the operating parameters, including temperature, pH, and feed solution concentration on cation separation has been extensively investigated [8,

Conversely, only a few studies have focused on coupling the fabrication conditions of NF membranes with the DSPM-DE model [21–23]. These studies generally performed sensitivity analyses by varying one or two DSPM-DE input parameters at a time. For example, Labban et al. [23] fabricated membranes using layer-by-layer deposition and performed a sensitivity analysis to opti­mize a membrane for a desalination pretreatment. Furthermore, L´eniz-Pizarro et al. [22] synthesized NF membranes using interfacial polymerization (IP) between piperazine (PIP) and trimesoyl chloride (TMC) with the addition of polyallylamine hydrochloride (PAH) to yield positively charged membranes for the selective separation of multiva­lent cations. They utilized the DSPM-DE model to better understand the effect of the addition of PAH on the separation focusing mainly on cx and εpore. However, these studies reveal a gap in understanding the combined effects of all membrane physicochemical characteristics within the context of NF membrane fabrication. Our study addresses this gap by systematically varying all four DSPM-DE input parameters through changes in the fabrication conditions. Unlike our previous study of anion separation [5], the selective transport mechanisms governing Na+ and Mg2+ separation, where Donnan becomes a dominant factor, have remained less explored.

content: ration [5], the selective transport mechanisms governing Na+ and Mg2+ separation, where Donnan becomes a dominant factor, have remained less explored. By exploring the correlations between the DSPM-DE input parameters, the partitioning mechanisms, and trans­membrane molar fluxes of Na+ and Mg2+, we provide a comprehensive assessment of the impact of intrinsic membrane characteristics on cation selectivity. Most commercial NF membranes are fabricated by IP. They are thinfilm composite (TFC) membranes, comprising a thin polyamide (PA) active layer on top of a porous support [24]. Among the different IP reaction conditions, the monomer concentrations play a crucial role in dictating the final characteristics of the membranes, altering the thick­ness, porosity, surface charge, and roughness of the active layer [2,5,25–36]. Thus, varying the monomer concentrations during IP is ex­pected to affect the four DSPM-DE input parameters of the resulting membranes, considering their concurrent influence [5,35,36]. Following our earlier work on NO-3 /SO2-4 separation [5], this study extends the investigation to the selective separation of Na+ and Mg2+ ions. In this study, for the first time, we investigated the influence of the concentrations of the monomers PIP and TMC on the Mg2+/Na+ selec­tivity of the TFC NF membranes using the DSPM-DE model.

content: stigated the influence of the concentrations of the monomers PIP and TMC on the Mg2+/Na+ selec­tivity of the TFC NF membranes using the DSPM-DE model. We sys­tematically varied the concentrations of the monomers to alter the membranes’ physicochemical characteristics and evaluated their sepa­ration performance with PEG 200, single salt (NaCl, MgCl2, Na2SO4, and MgSO4), and mixed salt retention tests (NaCl + MgCl2 and Na2SO4 +MgSO4). For the first time, we provide a mechanistic understanding of the phenomena underlying Mg2+ and Na + separation in terms of PIP and TMC concentration by correlating the membranes’ physicochemical features with the DSPM-DE input parameters. These correlations eluci­dated the role of the membranes’ characteristics in their performance, offering new insights into the partitioning and transmembrane ion fluxes. Unlike our earlier work focusing on NO-3 /SO2-4 separation [5], this study provides novel insights into how PIP and TMC concentrations affect Na+/Mg2+ separation in TFC NF membranes. This knowledge is crucial for optimizing membrane fabrication conditions and designing NF membranes specifically targeted for efficient Na+/Mg2+ separation. Efficient separation of Mg2+ from Na+ is vital, as magnesium is considered a “critical raw material” by the European Commission due to its essential and sustainable supply [37]. 2. Fabrication of PA membranes TFC membranes consisting of a PA active layer on top of a porous PAN support were fabricated by IP with aqueous PIP and organic TMC solutions of varying concentrations. A minimum of three membranes was fabricated under each of the conditions presented in Table 1, which summarizes the monomer concentrations we used. One day before the synthesis of the TFC membranes, the PAN support was immersed in ultrapure DI water; PIP and TMC were dissolved in ultrapure DI water and HPLC-grade n-heptane, respectively. One-minute rubber rolling and dust-free Kim wipes were used to remove any droplets from the surface of the PAN supports. Then, a 6-min immersion of the support in 350 ml PIP solution was carried out. Next, any remaining droplets on top of the PAN support were removed by the previously mentioned procedure.

The supports were clamped onto a 5.1 cm * 15.9 cm glass plate, with a frame consisting of one layer of nitrile rubber and one PTFE frame. Then, the TMC solutions were poured on top of the soaked PAN supports to start the 90-s IP reactions. The IP reaction was stopped by pouring 150 ml of pure n-heptane. Then, the TFC membranes were air-dried for 30 min at room temperature, cured for 30 min at 60 ◦C under vacuum, and finally stored in ultrapure DI water. 2.2.2. Characterization The membrane morphology was analyzed with scanning electron microscopy (SEM). SEM images were captured with a Merlin SEM (Zeiss, Jena, Germany) at accelerating voltages between 1.5 and 3 kV using an InLens secondary electron detector. Before imaging, the samples were dried under vacuum at 60 ◦C for at least 72 h and coated with 1–1.5 nm platinum using a CCU-010 coating device (Safematic, Zizers, Switzerland). The cross-fractured specimens were prepared in liquid nitrogen. The topography data of the membrane surfaces were obtained by an Atomic Force Microscopy MultiMode 8 instrument from Bruker, USA, in Tapping mode. Typical scan areas were 3 μm * 3 μm. RTESPA-150 probes (tip radius ≈8 nm, spring constant ≈5 N/m) were applied for these measurements. Roughness values Rq and Ra were calculated with the program NanoScope Analysis 1.9.

To fully solve the DSPM-DE model, the membranerelated input parameters, namely rpore, Δxe, εpore, and cx, need to be determined. They were determined by fitting retention test data. The details of the fitting procedure and the set of equations used to solve the model are described in the supporting information.

content: 3. Results and discussion 3.1. Membrane morphology and water permeance Cross-sections of the TFC membranes were investigated with SEM to determine the thickness of the PA layer (Figs. 1 and 2). The thickness of the PA layer increased with PIP concentration. The top surface of the prepared membranes was investigated both with SEM and AFM. The SEM micrographs demonstrate heterogeneous granular features, which are typical for PA layers [38]. The formation of such rough heteroge­neous surface is associated with the complex reaction kinetics of IP, monomer partitioning, and diffusion of monomers in the two-phase system. The surface roughness of the PA layer has long been corre­lated with the permeance of the membrane. A general intuition has been that the higher the surface roughness, the higher the permeance. By now, it is well established that although it is logical to claim that an increased interfacial area between the feed solution and membrane active layer can lead to high permeance, the irregular morphology of the PA layer has additional features that influence the permeance. The thickness of PA layers in TFC membranes varies significantly due to their inherent heterogeneity, which directly impacts local mass transport. This variation in thickness creates regions where the resistance to mass transport differs, leading to an uneven distribution of permeance across the membrane.

Journal of Membrane Science 721 (2025) 123808 contrast, regions of the PA layer that are farther from the substrate pores require a much longer path length for permeation, increasing resistance in these areas. Beneath the surface of the heterogeneous PA layers, there are regions with nanovoids. These nanovoids help mitigate the "funnel effect" by providing easier passage for permeating substances, enabling them to reach the substrate pores more efficiently. As a result, TFC membranes with PA layers exhibit localized regions of significantly higher permeance compared to neighboring areas. The DSPM-DE model considers that the selective layer of the nanofiltration membrane is composed of equally spaced perpendicularly aligned cylinders (Fig. 3b). Under this assumption, the influence of surface roughness on the per­meance is averaged out by the input parameter Δxe which represents the effective thickness-to-porosity ratio. We have explored the covariance and correlation of the variables RMS surface roughness (obtained from AFM), average observed thickness (obtained from SEM), rpore (obtained from PEG retention), Δxe (obtained from PEG retention) and pure water permeance (PWP). Both roughness and thickness of the PA layer increased with PIP concentration for the membranes prepared using TMC concentration 0.15 w-w% (Figs. 1) and 0.3 w-w% (Fig. 2). Consequently, a positive covariance is observed between the roughness and the thickness of the membranes (Fig.

content: 5 w-w% (Figs. 1) and 0.3 w-w% (Fig. 2). Consequently, a positive covariance is observed between the roughness and the thickness of the membranes (Fig. 3c). In other words, the higher roughness of the membranes comes at the expense of a greater overall thickness of the PA layer. The PWP increases with the roughness as expected. Additionally, a positive covariance is observed between PWP and roughness (Fig. 3d). This suggests that the roughness of the PA layers plays a more dominant role in determining the resistance of the membranes to water permeation compared to overall thickness of the PA layer. This conclusion is supported by the fact that an increase in overall thickness might also imply an increase in resistance to water transport, which would typically lead to a decrease in PWP. Therefore, the observed increase in PWP with roughness indicates that roughness is the more influential factor in dictating PWP. It is also observed that PWP decreases with increasing rpore which is counterintuitive. It suggests while rpore is representative of the size sieving feature of the membranes, it does not dictate the resistance against mass transport through the membranes. The rpore values of all the membranes range between 0.6 and 0.7 nm. In other words, the obtained rpore values are rather similar to each other and rpore cannot explain the resistance of the membranes against permeation of water.

In this work, we have thoroughly investigated both single and mixed salt retention behavior to understand the role of co-ions and counterions on the ideal and real selectivity of the membranes. The influence of Cl-as counterion on the Na+/Mg2+ ideal and real selectivities of the membranes is depicted in Fig. 4. Single salt retention tests results show that the Mg2+ rejection is larger than the Na + rejection for all the membranes fabricated at a PIP concentration larger than 0.5% w-w regardless of the TMC concentration. The rejection of Na+ is higher than that of Mg2+ for PIP0.125TMC0.15, PIP0.125 TMC0.3 and PIP0.25TMC0.3 membranes. This is also observed in the values of the ideal selectivity being smaller than 1 (Fig. 4c). Therefore, it was expected that PIP0.125TMC0.15, PIP0.125 TMC0.3, and PIP0.25TMC0.3 membranes would allow faster permeation of Mg2+ compared to Na+ when the feed solution contains a mixture of NaCl and MgCl2, i.e., a ternary ion mixture of Na+, Mg2+ and Cl-. However, that was not the case. The rejection of Mg2+ is larger than that of Na+ for all the membranes, highlighting the selective separation of the membranes. The rejection of Na+ even reaches negative values, which generally become more negative as PIP concentration increases.

Na+ and Mg2+ ions were almost completely retained (i.e., 100% retention) by the membranes when the feed solutions contained Na2SO4 and MgSO4 single salts, respectively (Fig. 5a). From our detailed investigation of retention and selectivities of the membranes using feed solutions containing binary and ternary ion systems, it is evident that the ideal and real Na+/Mg2+ selectivities are substantially different from each other. The retention of the ions by membranes is pictured as a combi­nation of three steps: (i) partitioning of the ions at the interface between feed solution and membrane, (ii) ion transport through the membrane, and (iii) partitioning of the ions at the interface between membrane and permeate solution. According to the DSPM-DE model, the ion parti­tioning at the interfaces occurs as a result of three exclusion mecha­nisms: (i) steric, (ii) dielectric, and (iii) Donnan (Fig. 6a). The partitioning of ions at the interface between feed solution and the membrane is expressed as [6]: ci,1γi,1 = ci,mγi,mΦS,iΦx=0D,i ΦDE,i(5) where ci,1 is the concentration of ion i at the pore entrance on the feed side; γi,1 and γi,m are the activity coefficients of ion i at the pore entrance on the feed side and in the feed-solution/membrane interface, respec­tively. ΦS,i is the steric exclusion coefficient, ΦDE,i is the dielectric exclusion coefficient and Φx=0D,i is the Donnan exclusion coefficient. The product of these terms (i.e., ΦS,iΦx=0D,i ΦDE,i) is termed as the overall exclusion coefficient.

The bare ionic radius (also termed as crystal ionic radius) of Cl- (1.81 Å) > Na+ (0.95 Å) > Mg2+ (0.65 Å) [39,40]. Owing to the ion-water affinity, it is essential to take into account the radii of the hydrated ions rather than the bare ions to interpret the mass transport phenomenon. In general, ions with smaller bare ionic radius and higher charge have larger hydrated ionic radius compared to those having larger crystal ionic radius and smaller charge. By convention, Stokes radii are used as input parameters in DSPM-DE models as a measure of the size of the hydrated ions.

The Stokes radius of Mg2+ (3.47 Å) > Na+ (1.84 Å) > Cl-(1.21 Å) [39,40]. The Stokes ionic radii are calculated from the limiting ionic equivalent conductance. It essentially means that the mobility of an ion together with its neighboring water molecules (which have the same mobility as the ion) is translated into the size of the hydrated ion. The Stokes ionic radii are often referred to as the radius of a hard sphere where the ion and the neighboring water mol­ecules are considered as a part of the same entity. Therefore, Mg2+, Na+, and Cl-are schematically represented as a purple, green, and blue sphere in Fig. 7, respectively. All of the prepared membranes have rpore larger than the Stokes radius of the 3 ions. Therefore, ΦS,i of the 3 ions are smaller than 1. In other words, the pore size of the membrane is not small enough to completely exclude the ions as a result of steric hin­drance. Mg2+ has the lowest value of the steric exclusion coefficient due to its largest Stokes radius among the 3 ions. The ΦS,i of the 3 ions in­crease linearly with the increase of pore size as result of the change of PIP and TMC concentration during IP. N. Cevallos-Cueva et al. Journal of Membrane Science 721 (2025) 123808 moments, reorient surrounding the ion in response to the electric field. Consequently, the electrostatic forces between two neighboring ions are largely reduced in an aqueous medium. In other words, water is a strong dielectric medium. The dielectric constant of bulk water (i.e., the rela­tive permittivity) is approximately 80 [6]. The dielectric constant of the water is largely reduced inside the pores of a NF membrane, i.e., the reorientation of water molecules is limited due to spatial confinement. It is difficult for the water molecules inside the pores to solvate an ion compared to the molecules of bulk water. Thus, it is energetically favorable for the ions to stay in the bulk feed solution, which is a region of larger dielectric constant, than the narrow pores. In order to enter the confined region having a lower dielectric constant (i.e., the pores of the membrane), the ions have to overcome a solvation energy barrier. The DSPM-DE model takes into account the exclusion of ions at the mem­brane feed solution interfase, originating from this phenomenon, using the dielectric exclusion coefficient ΦDE,i.

Since Mg2+ is excluded the most by these two membranes (Fig. 9c), the concentration profiles of Cl-and Na+ are parallel (supporting information Figure SI14) to reach electroneutrality condi­tions within the membranes’ active layers. This indicates that their concentration gradients are coupled, suggesting that JNa+ is determined by JCl-,diff. Consequently, membranes PIP1TMC0.15 and PIP1TMC0.30 attain the largest values of Na+/Mg2+ selectivity among all the fabri­cated membranes.

Considering all these factors, between the two positively-charged membranes, PIP1TMC0.15 achieves the largest selectivity due to its stronger Donnan exclusion owing to its largest positive cx value (Fig. 9a). 4. Conclusions In this study, we assessed the effect of the concentrations of PIP and TMC during IP on the Na+/Mg2+ selective separation performance of TFC membranes having a PA selective layer, based on the DSPM-DE model. The effect of PIP and TMC concentrations on the Na+/ Mg2+ separation capabilities of the membranes was particularly dictated by Δ xe and cx. Our findings corroborate that Δxe, comprising the porosity, tortu­ osity, actual thickness (observed by SEM), and roughness of the PA layer (obtained from AFM), is a measure of the mass transport resistance of the membranes. PWP tests revealed that the roughness of the PA layer plays a dominant role in determining the PWP values of the fabricated membranes. Although the actual thickness of the PA layer contributes to the overall resistance, it is less significant than the roughness. Addi­tionally, Δxe was found to correspond with the magnitude of the diffu­sive fluxes. Interestingly, the diffusion of the most mobile and smallest ion, Cl-, dictated the magnitude of the spontaneously generated electric field.

content: e fluxes. Interestingly, the diffusion of the most mobile and smallest ion, Cl-, dictated the magnitude of the spontaneously generated electric field. To attain zero current conditions, the generated electric field impaired Cl-transport as the primary effect and accelerated Na+ and Mg2+ transport through their electromigrative fluxes. As a result, Δxe played a key role in determining the transmembrane ionic fluxes. The difference in the transmembrane fluxes between Na+ and Mg2+ primarily arose from the different impact of PIP and TMC concentrations on both diffusive and electromigrative fluxes. The separation between Na+ and Mg2+ was mainly impacted by cx, which governs the Donnan exclusion coefficients at the pore entrance. In general, the cx values became more positive or less negative with higher PIP and lower TMC concentrations. Specifically, the positively charged membranes ach­ieved the highest Na+/Mg2+ selectivity among all the prepared mem­branes. The concentrations of Na+ and Mg2+ inside the active layers of the positively charged membranes were lower compared to the nega­tively charged membranes. Consequently, their electromigrative and advective fluxes decreased, but their diffusive fluxes dominated the overall transmembrane transport from the feed to the permeate side. The coupling of Cl-and Na+ diffusion was necessary to attain zero current conditions.

Accordingly, the most positively-charged mem­brane, PIP1TM0.15, demonstrated the highest Na+/Mg2+real selectivity.
